text: Hello, I'm sharing an RTAP for a primary epigastric supraumbilical hernia. This is just above the umbilicus.
The video shows a large mass of incarcerated omentum visible within the hernia defect.
I debated about doing an ETAP with a retrorectus versus a TAP. And in the end, after discussing with some colleagues of mine, I chose on just doing a tap and I'm pretty happy with my decision.
The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap several centimeters lateral to the camera, near the median fat pad. The peritoneum appears very thin during this process.

This part of the dissection does take a little bit of time to get used to, it is not like tap for an inguinal where you have more robust peritoneum and transverse salis fascia in the location below the arcuate line. And so this can be challenging.
The surgeon uses their left hand to hold the peritoneal flap while carefully dissecting across to the contralateral side, avoiding grabbing the flap directly to prevent creating holes.
The surgeon begins dissecting across the peritoneal flap, aiming for the median fat pad. As the dissection progresses to the contralateral side, the tissue separates easily with blunt dissection.

But for here, you really do need to kind of do some sharp dissection and you can make a lot of holes here, which is frustrating at the start of the case. So being careful here is probably the most important part during the start of these procedures.
The surgeon separates the peritoneum from the posterior sheath by taking down the visible adventitial fibers between these structures.

The surgeon uses their left hand to push down on the peritoneal flap, carefully avoiding grabbing it to prevent tearing. As they reach the median fat pad, the dissection progresses easily to the contralateral side. The flap is opened along the length of the incision for maximum exposure.

The surgeon adjusts the pocket size by extending it inferiorly, using the left hand to push down while performing blunt and sharp dissection with the right hand. Care is taken not to grab the peritoneum, instead pushing down and dissecting within the created space.
The surgeon dissects above and below the hernia defect, creating a visible 'volcano sign' that aids in the safe and efficient reduction of the hernia.
The surgeon identifies the area of the umbilicus, where a small umbilical hernia is visible and has just been reduced.
The surgeon stops the dissection upon losing the median fat on the contralateral side, then proceeds to take down and reduce the hernia defect while carefully keeping the peritoneum intact.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using scissors to separate the thin peritoneum from the sticky posterior sheath, aiming to avoid creating holes. As the dissection progresses laterally, the tissue separation resembles the technique used in transversus abdominis release (TAR).

The surgeon gently pushes down to reveal adventitial tissue, creating a pocket large enough to accommodate a fifteen by fifteen mesh for the hernia repair, considering the defect size and patient dimensions.

The surgeon uses a Marlex mesh and dissects further laterally on the contralateral side to create redundancy in the flap. The dissection progresses easily as the peritoneum becomes very thin, allowing entry into the transversalis fascia if needed. The flap is expanded to ensure it can be sutured closed without tension.
Occasionally I will add a tap block to these cases. I rarely send patients home with narcotics and it is surprising how well they do.

The surgeon places a fifteen by fifteen mesh into the abdominal cavity and begins suturing the defect using a zero V-lock suture in a transverse orientation. The umbilical pseudosec is imbricated to create an 'innie' appearance and prevent seroma formation. The insufflation pressure is reduced to eight for the closure process.
And I left this Nearly completely unedited other than camera cleaning just so that you can see the process. There are a lot of ways to do it. This is just mine.

But every time I do one of these repairs I'm just really happy and want to share. And this was for that smaller umbilical defect. I have no idea if this stitch matters at all, or if it would hold, even if it did matter, but I had the length of the suture. So I decided to put a simple interrupted in that location.

The surgeon places a 3-0 silk suture at the center of the mesh before inserting it into the abdomen, positioning it through the center of the hernia defect. The mesh is centered within the flap, ensuring coverage of five to seven centimeters around the defect. The surgeon then secures the mesh using cardinal corner sutures.

The surgeon manipulates the mesh on the ipsilateral side, ensuring it is positioned against the interior abdominal wall. The mesh is pulled up to prevent folding, and corner sutures may be placed through the mesh to secure it.

The surgeon grabs the mesh to hold it up against the abdominal wall, then suspends the large hernia sacs to prevent them from flopping down into the abdomen. An NG catheter is inserted into the flap to reduce gas before removing the ports.
